questions.prompt: Select the best summary: 'Global classrooms improve collaboration when students compare local examples and discuss differences respectfully.'
options: Respectful comparison of local contexts strengthens collaboration.
Only local examples matter in education.
Collaboration is unrelated to discussion style.
questions.rationale: Option B captures both comparison and respectful discussion.

questions.prompt: Choose the strongest inference: 'Most students who practiced timed mock tests improved their completion rate.'
options: Timed practice can help completion speed for many students.
Mock tests reduce accuracy for all students.
Completion rate is impossible to improve.
questions.rationale: The statement supports a likely benefit for many, not all.

questions.prompt: Argument: 'Our online support hours should match one time zone only, because most users live there.' What is the strongest critique?
options: It ignores access equity for minority time zones.
It is too expensive to change support hours.
Time zones never affect student outcomes.
Most users should always be prioritized in every policy.
questions.rationale: The policy may exclude global learners and reduce fair access.

questions.prompt: A developer wrote the following commit message. Which option best describes what's wrong with it?
options: It's too vague — doesn't describe what was fixed or why.
It should be in past tense.
questions.rationale: Good commit messages explain the 'what' and 'why' so teammates can understand changes without reading every diff.

questions.prompt: DEBUG is to ERROR as REFACTOR is to ___
questions.rationale: Debugging addresses errors; refactoring addresses code smells (structural issues).

questions.prompt: The sentence 'The data shows that users prefers dark mode' is grammatically correct.
questions.rationale: 'Users' is plural, so the verb should be 'prefer' not 'prefers'. Correct: 'The data shows that users prefer dark mode.'

questions.prompt: Read the passage and identify the logical fallacy:
questions.context: "Everyone on our team uses Vim, so Vim must be the best editor for all developers everywhere."
questions.rationale: Generalizing from one team's preference to all developers is a hasty generalization — the sample is too small and biased.

questions.prompt: You have 5 sentences that form a logical paragraph, but they're shuffled. Which is the correct order?
questions.context:
A: Tests caught the regression before it reached users.
B: The team pushed a refactor to the authentication module.
C: A subtle bug was introduced in the session validation logic.
D: The CI pipeline ran the full test suite automatically.
E: The developer opened a fix PR within 30 minutes.
questions.rationale: Logical flow: team pushes refactor (B) → bug introduced (C) → CI runs tests (D) → tests catch it (A) → developer fixes (E).
